message.content:
Adversarial cryptography reviewer: REFUTE this candidate; default refuted=true unless solid. Read the real cb-mpc code at C:/bugBounty/targets/cb-mpc first.

CANDIDATE: Batch PVE decrypt: attacker-controlled inner Q vector length is not re-checked against batch_count, causing out-of-bounds std::vector read of ecc_point_t (wild EC_POINT* deref) on the honest decryptor
api: coinbase::api::pve::decrypt_batch(curve_id, dk, ek, ciphertext, label, out_xs) in include/cbmpc/api/pve_batch_single_recipient.h
class: Out-of-bounds read / missing length validation on deserialized container severity: Medium
file: src/cbmpc/protocol/pve_batch.cpp:183
missing_check: After deserializing ec_pve_batch_t from the untrusted blob, nothing verifies that the inner length-prefixed Q vector (read by ec_pve_batch_t::convert -> converter.convert(Q,...)) has size == n. ec_pve_batch_t::convert (include-internal/cbmpc/internal/protocol/pve_batch.h:48-61) checks `if (int(Q.size()) != n)` BEFORE reading, when Q is still the constructor-sized vector, so the check is a no-op; the subsequent std::vector overload does Q.clear()/resize(attacker_count), making Q.size() fully attacker-controlled. verify() (pve_batch.cpp:78) does enforce Q.size()==n, but the decrypt path runs with skip_verify=true and never re-validates. restore_from_decrypted() validates row_index but not Q.size().
data_flow: decrypt_batch -> parse_batch_ciphertext sets n = blob.batch_count (separate outer field, bounded by MAX_BATCH_COUNT) -> ec_pve_batch_t pve_ct(n) (constructor resizes Q to n) -> coinbase::convert(pve_ct, blob.ct) -> ec_pve_batch_t::convert: pre-check Q.size()==n passes (still n), then converter.convert(Q,...) clears and resizes Q to the attacker's inner length prefix m (m can be < n) -> get_Qs() curve loop iterates only the m present entries (no rejection) -> pve_ct.decrypt(..., skip_verify=true) -> for each kappa row, base_pke.decrypt(dk, inner_label, rows[i].c, x_buf): attacker can craft rows[i].c as a valid base-PKE ciphertext under the public ek so this returns SUCCESS for some row -> restore_from_decrypted(i, x_buf, curve, xs): builds x0,x1 of size n via bn_t::vector_from_bin, then loop `for (int i=0;i<n;i++){ x[i]=x0[i]+x1[i]; if (Q[i] != x[i]*G) ... }` reads Q[i] for i up to n-1 while Q.size()==m<n -> OOB std::vector<ecc_point_t> element whose embedded EC_POINT* pointer is uninitialized/out-of-bounds heap memory, then dereferenced inside ecc_point_t::operator!= (OpenSSL EC_POINT_cmp).
attacker_role: Malicious party that produces the batch-PVE ciphertext blob consumed by an honest decryptor via the public decrypt_batch API.
impact: A malicious counterparty who supplies the serialized batch-PVE ciphertext can force the honest party's decrypt_batch (or get_public_keys_compressed_batch / decrypt_batch_*_hsm, which share the same construct+deserialize pattern) to read past the end of the Q vector and dereference a wild EC_POINT* in OpenSSL. At minimum this is a remotely triggerable crash (DoS) of the honest decryptor; because the wild pointer is consumed as an OpenSSL EC_POINT object, the concrete impact ranges from segfault to controlled OOB read depending on heap layout. No honest-party secret is leaked, but it is reachable purely through the public API boundary with valid base-PKE rows.
excerpt: // pve_batch.h convert(): pre-check is a no-op, then convert(Q,...) resizes Q to attacker count
if (int(Q.size()) != n) { converter.set_error(); return; }
converter.convert(Q, L, b);
// pve_batch.cpp restore_from_decrypted: loops to n, indexes attacker-sized Q
for (int i = 0; i < n; i++) { MODULO(q) x[i] = x0[i] + x1[i]; if (Q[i] != x[i] * G) return coinbase::error(E_CRYPTO); }

LENS = PUBLIC-API REACHABILITY. Trace that the code is reached from include/cbmpc/api/ (not only demos/c_api). If not, refuted=true, reachable_via_public_api=false.

is_real=true only if it survives against the real code. Return ONLY the structured object.

- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- contribute:contribute: This skill should be used when the user asks to "contribute to open source", "find an issue to work on", "analyze a repo", "submit a pull request", "review PR feedback", "triage an issue", "sync my fork", "create a release", "clean up contribution branches", "debug CI failures", or mentions open-source contribution workflows. Provides a full-lifecycle open-source contribution system with 12 phases.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text: The candidate's core claim hinges on `converter.convert(Q, ...)` resizing Q to an attacker-controlled length. Let me check the converter's behavior for `std::vector<ecc_point_t>`.

The candidate's key claim is that `converter.convert(Q, L, b)` resizes Q to an attacker-controlled length. The pre-check `if (int(Q.size()) != n)` happens while Q is still constructor-sized n, so it passes. Then convert deserializes Q. I need to verify the converter behavior for `std::vector<ecc_point_t>`.

input.command: grep -rn "convert" C:/bugBounty/targets/cb-mpc/include-internal/cbmpc/core/convert.h 2>/dev/null | grep -i "vector\|ecc_point\|resize" | head -50; echo "==="; find C:/bugBounty/targets/cb-mpc -name "convert*.h" -o -name "convert*.cpp" | head

content.text:
Confirmed: when deserializing (`!write`), `value.clear()` then `value.resize(count)` where count is the attacker-controlled length prefix. So the pre-check `if (int(Q.size()) != n)` in `ec_pve_batch_t::convert` is indeed a no-op against the deserialized size — Q.size() becomes attacker-controlled m.

This confirms the candidate's central mechanism. Now I must verify the LENS: is this reachable from `include/cbmpc/api/`? Let me check the actual decrypt_batch API path and whether it re-validates Q.size().

This is the crux. The candidate claims that after `coinbase::convert(pve_ct, blob.ct)`, Q.size() can be attacker-controlled m != n, and nothing re-validates. Let me check carefully.

There IS a loop at line 165-167 after convert: iterating over `pve_ct.get_Qs()` checking curve. But that only iterates the m present entries — it doesn't check size==n. So if m < n, this loop passes (all m entries valid curve), and then decrypt runs.

But wait — the candidate claims `m can be < n`. Let me check the `ec_pve_batch_t::convert` pre-check again. The pre-check `if (int(Q.size()) != n)` is on the constructor-sized Q (=n), so passes. Then `converter.convert(Q, L, b)` deserializes — which clears and resizes Q to attacker count m. So after convert, `pve_ct.get_Qs().size() == m`, but `pve_ct.batch_count() == n` (n is a separate member, not reset).

Then decrypt → restore_from_decrypted loops `for(i=0;i<n;i++) Q[i]` while Q.size()==m. If m<n: OOB read. Let me verify `n` is not re-derived from Q. In convert, `n` is the member set by constructor, never updated. Confirmed.

Now the critical question for severity: can the attacker actually reach restore_from_decrypted with a SUCCESS base_pke.decrypt? The decrypt loop tries each kappa row; if base_pke.decrypt fails, it continues. restore_from_decrypted is only called on decrypt SUCCESS. Even if restore returns error, it's only reached when decrypt succeeds. But actually — the OOB happens inside restore_from_decrypted regardless of whether base_pke validated, as long as decrypt of that row's c succeeded. Let me check whether the attacker controls rows[i].c — yes, fully (deserialized). With the public ek, can they craft a valid base-PKE ciphertext? They'd need it to decrypt under the honest party's dk. Actually base_pke.decrypt with dk — attacker doesn't have dk, but can produce a ciphertext that decrypts to *something* (RSA-OAEP/ECIES generally either decrypt to a value or fail). 

Actually, even simpler: the OOB read also could occur via the `get_Qs()` checks. But let me check: is there a size check somewhere I'm missing? The verify() path checks Q.size()==n but decrypt uses skip_verify=true. Let me confirm there's truly no re-check of get_Qs().size()==n in the API. Looking at the decrypt_batch API: lines 165-167 iterate get_Qs() but never compare size to n. Confirmed no size check.

Let me verify the bn_t::vector_from_bin sizing and whether restore reaches the Q[i] loop. Note x0,x1 are sized to n (from vector_from_bin with count n), so x[i] for i<n is fine; only Q[i] is the OOB. Let me confirm vector_from_bin.

x0/x1 are sized to n correctly. The only OOB is `Q[i]` for i in [m, n). The candidate's analysis holds against the real code:

1. `convert` pre-check is a no-op (Q still sized n at check time), then resize to attacker count m. ✓
2. `n` member is separate, bounded by MAX_BATCH_COUNT, never re-derived from Q. ✓
3. decrypt uses skip_verify=true, so verify()'s Q.size()==n check is bypassed. ✓
4. API loop `for (auto& q : get_Qs())` checks curve but NOT size==n. ✓
5. restore_from_decrypted loops `Q[i]` to n while Q.size()==m. ✓
6. Reachable via public API `coinbase::api::pve::decrypt_batch` in include/cbmpc/api/. ✓

The one nuance: restore_from_decrypted is only reached if `base_pke.decrypt` succeeds for a row. But there's a subtlety — even if the attacker can't make decrypt succeed, can they trigger the OOB? No — only on decrypt SUCCESS. However, an attacker can take a legitimately-encrypted valid blob (where decrypt of at least one row succeeds), then tamper ONLY the Q length prefix to be m<n while leaving rows intact. The rows still decrypt fine (rows are independent of Q's serialization beyond the inner_label). 

Wait — `inner_label = genPVELabelWithPoint(label, Q)` is computed from Q (now size m). The base_pke ciphertext was encrypted with inner_label derived from the full Q (size n). If Q changed, inner_label changes, so base_pke.decrypt (which is label-bound) would fail. Let me check genPVELabelWithPoint and whether label mismatch causes decrypt failure.

The label binding is a real obstacle for the "tamper an honest blob" variant, but it does NOT save the bug. The attacker controls the ENTIRE blob (they are the malicious party producing it). They can:
- Set Q to size m with values matching some target points
- Compute inner_label = genPVELabelWithPoint(label, Q_with_size_m) themselves
- Craft rows[i].c as valid base_pke encryptions under the public ek using THAT inner_label

base_pke.decrypt(dk, inner_label, c) — the attacker encrypted c under ek with the label they control, so it decrypts successfully under dk. The attacker doesn't need dk to *encrypt* under ek (ek is public). So they can make at least one row decrypt successfully → restore_from_decrypted → OOB read of Q[m..n).

This is a real, attacker-constructible bug reachable purely via the public API. The label binding is consistent because the attacker computes everything from the size-m Q.

Let me just confirm base_pke encrypt only needs the public ek (so attacker can craft valid c).

Confirmed. base_pke encrypt uses the public ek; the attacker can craft valid ciphertexts under ek with the inner_label they compute from their chosen size-m Q. Decryption under dk succeeds with the matching label. So restore_from_decrypted is reachable, and the `Q[i]` loop reads OOB for i in [m, n).

The bug is real, reachable through the public API `coinbase::api::pve::decrypt_batch`. The OOB read of `ecc_point_t` then dereferences an uninitialized/wild `EC_POINT*` in `operator!=` (EC_POINT_cmp). This is at minimum a DoS, reachable across the public API trust boundary from a malicious counterparty's ciphertext blob. Medium severity is appropriate.

The candidate's analysis is accurate and survives against the real code. I will NOT refute it.

input.attack_at_boundary: Malicious counterparty supplies the serialized batch-PVE ciphertext blob consumed by an honest decryptor via coinbase::api::pve::decrypt_batch (and the HSM variants / get_public_keys_compressed_batch / get_Label_batch), which is the public API trust boundary in include/cbmpc/api/.
input.reason: Verified against real code. ec_pve_batch_t::convert (pve_batch.h:48-61) does `if (int(Q.size()) != n) set_error()` while Q is still constructor-sized n, so the check is a no-op; then converter.convert(Q,...) for std::vector<T> (convert.h:114-131) does value.clear()/value.resize(count) with count = attacker length prefix (bounded only by MAX_CONTAINER_ELEMENTS=1<<20), so Q.size() becomes attacker-controlled m, while the member n stays = blob.batch_count (bounded by MAX_BATCH_COUNT). decrypt_batch (pve_batch_single_recipient.cpp:139-187) calls decrypt with skip_verify=true, bypassing verify()'s Q.size()==n check (pve_batch.cpp:78). The post-convert loop `for (auto& q : pve_ct.get_Qs())` (line 165) only iterates the m present entries and checks curve, never size==n. decrypt -> restore_from_decrypted (pve_batch.cpp:181-184) loops `for (i=0;i<n;i++) if (Q[i] != x[i]*G)` reading Q[i] up to n-1 while Q.size()==m; for m<n this is an OOB std::vector<ecc_point_t> read whose embedded EC_POINT* is wild, then dereferenced in ecc_point_t::operator!= (EC_POINT_cmp). restore_from_decrypted is only reached on base_pke.decrypt SUCCESS, but the attacker fully controls the blob: ek is public, inner_label = genPVELabelWithPoint(label, Q) is computed from the attacker's size-m Q, so the attacker can craft rows[i].c as a valid base-PKE ciphertext under ek with that exact label, which decrypts successfully under the honest dk -> reaches the OOB. Reachable purely through the public include/cbmpc/api/ boundary. Impact: remotely triggerable crash/OOB read (DoS) of honest decryptor; no secret leak. Medium is appropriate.
